text.title: JWT Patterns: Claims, RS256, Short TTL, Refresh Rotation
text.summary: JWT payload requirements, RS256/EdDSA signing, access token TTL limits, refresh token rotation, and denylist approach.

rule.statement: Always verify iss, aud, exp, nbf with clock-skew tolerance of at most 60 s; reject tokens missing any of these.
rule.rationale: Missing audience check lets a token issued for service A be replayed at service B. Missing issuer check lets any party in the trust boundary mint tokens for your service.

example.note: jti enables denylist revocation. scope allows fine-grained permission checks.

rule.statement: Use RS256 or EdDSA for multi-service architectures; never HS256 with a shared secret across services.
rule.rationale: A shared HS256 secret means any service that holds it can mint admin-level tokens. RS256 lets services verify with the public key; only the auth service can sign.

rule.statement: Never pass algorithm=none or accept tokens without an explicit algorithms=[...] list in the decoder.
rule.rationale: alg:none is a historical CVE class; jwt.decode without explicit algorithms accepts it on some libraries.

rule.statement: Access token TTL must be at most 15 minutes; refresh tokens rotate on every use and must detect reuse.
rule.rationale: Short access TTL limits the blast radius of a stolen token. Refresh token rotation with reuse detection invalidates the entire refresh family on replay, catching stolen tokens.

rule.statement: For revocation: keep a Redis denylist keyed by jti with TTL equal to the remaining token lifetime.
rule.rationale: Stateless JWTs cannot be invalidated mid-life without a server-side record. A denylist with matching TTL avoids indefinite storage growth.

example.note: Explicit algorithms list, audience verification, and UTC-aware timestamps.

rule.statement: Store JWTs in HTTP-only SameSite=Lax cookies for SPAs; never in localStorage.
rule.rationale: localStorage is readable by any JavaScript on the page — XSS immediately steals all tokens. HTTP-only cookies are invisible to JS; SameSite=Lax blocks most CSRF.
